title: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat
authors: Robert P. Giugliano, Christopher P. Cannon, Eugene Braunwald

items.subtopic: Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction and Unstable Angina) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Understanding the Rising Incidence of NSTEMI in NSTE-ACS
items.content: The incidence of non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI) is increasing due to factors such as obesity, diabetes, and chronic kidney disease. This trend is also influenced by enhanced detection capabilities using highly sensitive troponin assays.
keyPoints: Incidence of NSTEMI is rising
Contributing factors include obesity, diabetes, chronic kidney disease
Enhanced detection with highly sensitive troponin assays reclassifies unstable angina to NSTEMI
items.reference: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat · Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction and Unstable Angina) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction and Unstable Angina) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
items.title: Pathophysiology of NSTE-ACS: Plaque Vulnerability and Thrombosis
items.content: NSTE-ACS is primarily caused by an imbalance between myocardial oxygen supply and demand, often due to coronary arterial thrombosis from vulnerable plaques. These plaques can fissure with or without inflammation or erode, contributing to the condition.
keyPoints: Imbalance between myocardial oxygen supply and demand
Causes include plaque fissure (with/without inflammation) and erosion
<10% of NSTE-ACS patients have left main coronary artery stenosis at angiography
Approximately 35% have three-vessel disease, 25% two-vessel disease, 20% single-vessel disease, and 10% no apparent critical epicardial coronary artery stenosis
items.reference: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat · Non-ST-Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (Non-ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction and Unstable Angina) | PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing NSTE-ACS: Key Features and Red Flags
items.content: NSTE-ACS diagnosis relies on history, clinical findings including age, ECG, and troponin levels. Chest discomfort in NSTE-ACS is severe with features like rest occurrence lasting >10 min, recent onset within 2 weeks, or a crescendo pattern. Anginal equivalents such as dyspnea, epigastric discomfort, nausea, or weakness are more common in women, the elderly, and diabetics. Physical examination may reveal signs of large myocardial ischemia or NSTEMI, including diaphoresis, pale skin, sinus tachycardia, heart sounds, rales, hypotension, and cardiogenic shock.
keyPoints: Chest discomfort features: rest occurrence >10 min, recent onset within 2 weeks, crescendo pattern
Anginal equivalents more common in women, elderly, diabetics
Physical exam signs of large ischemia/NSTEMI: diaphoresis, pale skin, sinus tachycardia, heart sounds, rales, hypotension, cardiogenic shock
items.reference: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Biomarkers for NSTEMI
items.content: NSTEMI is diagnosed in patients with chest discomfort features without ECG ST-segment elevations but with elevated troponin levels indicating myocardial necrosis. New ST-segment depression occurs in about one-third of NSTE-ACS cases, while T-wave changes are more common but less specific unless new and deep (≥0.3 mV). High-sensitive cardiac troponins are the preferred markers for myocardial necrosis, with a characteristic temporal rise peaking at 12–24 hours post-symptom onset. Elevated troponin levels correlate directly with mortality.
keyPoints: NSTEMI diagnosis: chest discomfort features + elevated troponin without ST-segment elevations
ST-segment depression in ~1/3 of NSTE-ACS; T-wave changes common but less specific unless new and deep (≥0.3 mV)
High-sensitive cardiac troponins preferred for myocardial necrosis, peak at 12–24 hours post-symptoms
Direct relationship between troponin elevation degree and mortality
items.reference: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat · CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Why Assess Risk in NSTE-ACS Patients?
items.content: Risk assessment in patients with Non-ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome (NSTE-ACS) is crucial for determining the early risk of death and recurrent ACS. This helps identify those who would benefit most from an early invasive strategy.
keyPoints: Early 30-day mortality risk ranges from 1 to 10%.
Recurrent ACS rate during the first year is 5–15%.
Risk can be assessed using clinical scoring systems like TIMI, GRACE, and HEART score.
Multimarker strategies including hsTn and brain natriuretic peptides are gaining favor for better risk stratification.
items.reference: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat · RISK STRATIFICATION

items.title: How Do Multimarker Strategies Enhance Risk Stratification in NSTE-ACS?
items.content: Multimarker strategies, including high-sensitivity troponin (hsTn) and brain natriuretic peptides, are increasingly used to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the pathophysiological mechanisms in NSTE-ACS patients. This approach aids in refining risk stratification.
keyPoints: Multimarker strategies help define pathophysiologic mechanisms underlying patient presentations.
These strategies further stratify patient risk beyond traditional scoring systems.
items.reference: Non ST Segment Elevation Acute Coronary Syndrome Non ST Segment Elevat · RISK STRATIFICATION

items.question: Which of the following processes is NOT typically involved in causing NSTE-ACS?
options: Fixed epicardial coronary obstruction without thrombosis
items.explanation: NSTE-ACS is caused by processes leading to coronary arterial thrombosis or increased myocardial oxygen demand, not by coronary arterial aneurysms.

items.question: What is the primary reason for the increasing incidence of NSTEMI?
options: Decreased use of aspirin and statins
Increased detection of myocardial necrosis by troponin
Reduced prevalence of diabetes and obesity
items.explanation: The incidence of NSTEMI is rising due to increased detection of myocardial necrosis by troponin.
